items.q: What are the four Ps of the marketing mix?
items.correct: Product, Price, Place (distribution), and Promotion — the controllable variables a business uses to satisfy customers and achieve objectives.
items.misconception: The four Ps are Product, Price, Profit, and People.
items.random_wrong: The four Ps are Planning, Producing, Promoting, and Profit.
items.why: Students often substitute 'profit' or 'people' into the four Ps. The classic McCarthy framework is Product, Price, Place and Promotion — extended models add others but those four are foundational.

items.correct: The level of output where total revenue equals total costs — neither profit nor loss. It is NOT the level where profit is maximised; profit grows beyond break-even.
items.misconception: Break-even is where the business makes the most profit.
items.random_wrong: Break-even is where the business owes no debt.
items.why: Break-even is a no-profit-no-loss point, useful for risk analysis. Students conflate it with profit maximisation. Going above break-even is where profit starts; below break-even means a loss.

items.q: What four growth strategies does Ansoff's Matrix identify?
items.correct: Market penetration (existing product, existing market), market development (existing product, new market), product development (new product, existing market), diversification (new product, new market).
items.misconception: Ansoff's Matrix is about analysing competitors.
items.random_wrong: Ansoff's Matrix shows market share against profit.
items.why: Students confuse Ansoff (growth strategy) with Porter's Five Forces (competition) or BCG Matrix (portfolio). Ansoff is specifically about the four product/market combinations for growth.

items.q: A WEAK pound is generally GOOD.
items.why: It's UK exporters. “UK importers” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: When interest rates rise, business investment usually.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Falls. “Rises” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Loan £40,000 at 5% interest for one year. What is the interest paid, in £?
items.why: The real figure is 2000. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
